questions.title: How often do you brush your teeth?

options.feedback: Brushing once a day is good, but it is recommended to brush twice a day to maintain optimal oral health.

options.optionLabel: I don't brush my teeth daily
options.feedback: It's important to brush at least once a day to remove plaque and prevent tooth decay.

questions.title: How often do you floss your teeth?

options.feedback: Good job! Try to increase flossing frequency to daily for best results.
Flossing occasionally is better than not at all. Aim to floss daily to improve your oral health
Flossing is essential for removing plaque between teeth. Start flossing daily to prevent gum disease.

questions.title: How often do you use a mouthrinse?

options.feedback: Good! Consider using it daily for maximum benefit.
Try to use mouth rinse more regularly to improve your oral health.
Adding a mouth rinse to your routine can help reduce plaque and improve oral hygiene.

questions.title: Which additional oral health aids do you use? (Check all that apply)

options.feedback: Manual toothbrushes are reliable for daily oral care. Make sure you use soft bristles to decrease your risk of damaging your gums.
Power toothbrushes improve brushing efficiency and effectiveness while being gentler along the gumline to promote great oral health.
String floss effectively removes plaque and debris from in between your teeth. Make sure to wrap it around each side of the tooth to adapt the floss to your gumline.
Floss picks are convenient for on-the-go use. While string floss can be more effective, a lot of people find that floss sticks are easier to use.
Soft picks are gentle yet effective, offering an easier solution for cleaning larger spaces, such as areas with bone loss or underneath bridges.
Water flossers effectively clean between teeth and along the gumline, ideal for braces and dental work.
The tongue harbors bacteria and is often overlooked in oral health routines. Using a tongue scraper can effectively clean the tongue and help control bacterial buildup.

questions.title: Do you grind or clench your teeth?

options.feedback: Grinding or clenching can lead to tooth damage and jaw pain. If you are experiencing these issues, consult your dentist.

If you're unsure, your dentist can check for signs of grinding or clenching during your next visit. Without preventative measures, clenching and grinding can cause excess wear on your teeth, strain on your jaw joints, and muscle pain.

questions.title: How often do your gums bleed when flossing?

options.feedback: Occasional bleeding can be a sign of gum inflammation. Ensure you're brushing and flossing properly.
Frequent bleeding is a sign of gum disease, which can lead to loss of supporting bone and tissues around your teeth over time. Visit your dentist for an evaluation.
Persistent bleeding is a sign of gum disease, which can lead to loss of supporting bone and tissues around your teeth over time. Visit your dentist for an evaluation.

questions.title: How many acidic foods or beverages do you consume in a day? (e.g., citrus fruits, carbonated beverages, wine, etc.)

options.feedback: Moderate consumption is better but try to limit acidic foods and drinks to protect your enamel.
Frequent consumption of acidic foods can erode tooth enamel. Rinse your mouth with water after consuming acidic items and consider using an enamel-protecting toothpaste to help.

questions.title: Do you experience tooth sensitivity to hot or cold?

options.feedback: Frequent sensitivity can indicate enamel erosion, gum recession, or other tooth-related issues. Consider using a toothpaste for sensitive teeth and consult your dentist.
Occasional sensitivity is common but can be a sign of other tooth-related issues. Consider using a toothpaste for sensitive teeth but if it persists, consult your dentist.

questions.title: If you have pain or discomfort in your jaw or in front of your ears (where the jaw opens and closes), how would you rate it?

options.feedback: Occasional jaw pain can often be helped with simple self-care. If it persists, consult your dentist.
Moderate pain should be evaluated by a dentist to identify and treat the underlying cause.
Frequent jaw or jaw-joint pain should be evaluated by a dentist or specialist, who can advise you on next steps.
Severe and persistent pain requires immediate attention. Schedule a dental appointment to address the issues

questions.title: Do you smoke or use any vaping products?

options.feedback: Smoking and vaping can impact your oral health and cause long-term irreversible damage. Consider quitting and discuss cessation options with your doctor.
Even occasional smoking and/or vaping can affect your oral health. Try to eliminate smoking or vaping completely.

questions.title: When was your last visit to a dental professional?

options.feedback: It's time for a check-up. Regular visits help prevent dental issues.
Schedule a dental appointment as soon as possible. Regular check-ups are crucial to maintaining your oral health.
It's important to start visiting the dentist. Schedule an appointment to begin your oral health care routine.

questions.title: Does the toothpaste you use most often contain fluoride?

options.feedback: Check the ingredient label for fluoride (often listed as sodium fluoride or similar). Fluoride toothpaste helps strengthen enamel and reduce decay risk.
Dental professionals typically recommend using fluoride toothpaste twice daily. For best results, ask your dental professional what's right for you.

questions.title: After brushing, what do you usually do?

options.optionLabel: Spit out excess toothpaste and rinse only briefly, or not at all
Rinse my mouth thoroughly with water
options.feedback: A full rinse washes away some fluoride left on the teeth. If your dental professional agrees, try spitting without a long rinse so fluoride stays in contact with your enamel a bit longer.
Ask your dental professional about rinsing habits. Many people benefit from fluoride toothpaste staying on the teeth a little longer after brushing.

questions.title: How many times per day do you have sugary snacks or sweetened drinks between meals (not counting regular meals)?

options.feedback: Frequent sugar exposure feeds bacteria that cause decay. Try to limit between-meal sweets and choose water when possible.
Many small exposures to sugar through the day can be hard on your teeth. Combine fewer snacks with brushing or rinsing with water, and see your dental professional for personalized advice.

questions.title: When you have a sugary or acidic drink or snack between meals, how do you usually consume it?

options.optionLabel: I rarely have these between meals
Mostly with a meal or finished within about 15 minutes
I sometimes sip or nibble over a longer period (e.g., 30+ minutes)
options.feedback: Sipping or grazing keeps acid and sugar in contact with teeth longer, which raises decay and erosion risk. Drinking with meals or finishing quicker is gentler on enamel.

options.optionLabel: I often sip or snack throughout much of the day
options.feedback: Constant exposure to sugar and acid is especially tough on teeth. When you can, have sweets or acidic drinks with a meal instead of on their own—eating with other foods. It helps to buffer acids and sugars and shortens the time your teeth are exposed.

questions.title: How often do you experience dry mouth?

options.feedback: Dry mouth can increase cavity risk and discomfort. Ask your dental professional about it at your next dental visit.
Persistent dry mouth often needs evaluation. Your dentistal professional can suggest strategies and tips to help you manage it.
Ongoing dry mouth raises decay and gum problems. Schedule a dental visit and discuss cause and strategies to help you manage it.

questions.title: On a typical day, how would you describe your water intake?

options.optionLabel: I drink water regularly throughout the day
I drink when thirsty but could be more consistent
I often feel I don't drink enough water
options.feedback: Water supports saliva and rinses food and acids from your mouth. Try keeping a bottle handy and sipping between meals.
Aim for steady water intake during the day; it helps oral comfort and buffers acids after eating and drinking.

questions.title: Would you say you're often a mouth breather—mostly breathing through your mouth when you're resting or sleeping?

options.optionLabel: No—not really. I mostly breathe through my nose when I'm resting or sleeping.
Sometimes—especially at night or when I'm very relaxed.
options.feedback: If this happens only sometimes, mouth breathing can still dry oral tissues and contribute to discomfort or bad breath. If it becomes more frequent, mention it to your dental professional.

options.optionLabel: Yes—I often breathe through my mouth when I'm resting or sleeping.
options.feedback: Regular mouth breathing while you rest or sleep may dry the mouth and increase dental risks. A dental professional can identify causes and options to protect your oral health.

questions.title: Roughly how long do you brush your teeth each time?

options.feedback: Aim for about two minutes each time so you can reach all surfaces. Try a timer or an electric brush with a built-in timer.
Short brushing often misses plaque along the gumline. Aim for about two minutes, twice a day, with gentle pressure.
Use a clock or phone timer for a few days to see how long you actually brush; many people underestimate. Aim for about two minutes each time so you can reach all surfaces.

questions.title: How often do you replace your toothbrush (or power brush head)?

options.optionLabel: About every 3-4 months, or sooner if bristles look frayed

options.feedback: Worn bristles clean less effectively. Replacing every 3-4 months or sooner if bristles look frayed is a good rule of thumb.

options.optionLabel: Less often than twice a year
options.feedback: Old or frayed brushes remove plaque poorly and can be harsh on gums. Plan to replace on a schedule you'll remember.

options.optionLabel: I'm not sure / only when I remember
options.feedback: Mark your calendar or set a reminder to replace your brush about every three to four months.
